Chunk # 0.
Journal of Neurochemistry 
Raven Press, Ltd., New York 
D 1990 International Society for Neurochemistry 
Rapid Isolation of Metabolically Active Mitochondria from 
Rat Brain and Subregions Using Percoll 
Density Gradient Centrifugation 
Neil R Sims 
Department of Medical Biochemistry and Centre for Neuroscience, Flinders University of South Australia, 
Bedsord Park, South Australia, Australia 
Abstract: Two procedures are described for isolating free 
(nonsynaptosomal) mitochondria from rat brain Both pro- 
cedures employ a discontinuous Percoll gradient and yield 
well coupled mitochondria which exhibit high rates of re- 
spiratory activity and contain little residual contamination 
by synaptosomes or myelin The procedures are considerably 
more rapid than methods described previously for the iso- 
lation of brain mitochondria and do not require an ultracen- 
trifuge or swing-out rotor The first method separates mito- 
chondria by gradient centrifugation from a P2 (crude mito- 
chondrial) fraction and is likely to be widely applicable for 
studies in which at least 500 mg of tissue are available as 
starting material In the second method, the unfractionated homogenate is subjected directly to gradient centrifugation 
This method requires the preparation of more gradients (per 
gram of tissue) than the first method and yields a subcellular 
fraction with slightly more synaptosomal contamination 
However, this second procedure is more rapid, requires less 
manipulation of the tissue, and is suitable for obtaining mi- 
tochondria with well preserved metabolic characteristics from 
subregions of single rat brains Key Words: Mitochondria- 
Oxidative phosphorylation-Percoll gradient centrifuga- 
tion-Rat brain-Subcellular fractionation Sims N R 
Rapid isolation of metabolically active mitochondria from 
rat brain and subregions using Percoll density gradient cen- 
trifugation J Neurochem 55, 698-707 (1990) 
Many early attempts to study mitochondria from 
mammalian brain were confounded by technical 
problems (see Clark and Nicklas, 1970, for references) 
Preparations adapted from those used for other tissues 
were heavily contaminated with several other subcel- 
lular components, particularly myelin and synapto- 
somes Removal of these contaminants using sucrose 
gradient centrifugation exposed the mitochondria to 
markedly hypertonic conditions, resulting in poor 
preservation of their metabolic properties The intro- 
duction of procedures for isolating free (i.e., nonsynap- 
tosomal) mitochondria by centrifugation through dis- 
continuous gradients of Ficoll overcame these problems 
(Stahl et al., 1963; Tanaka and Abood, 1963; Clark 
and Nicklas, 1970) Subsequently, variations of these 
procedures, including adaptations to improve the yield 
of free mitochondria and to allow simultaneous iso- 
lation of synaptosomal mitochondria (Lai and Clark, 
1976; Ginsberg et al., 1977; Lai et al., 1977), have been 
used in most studies where relatively uncontaminated 
metabolically active organelles are required Most of the commonly employed procedures for 
isolating mitochondria from rat brain were developed 
using the forebrains from several animals as starting 
material (Clark and Nicklas, 1970; Lai and Clark, 1976, 
1979; hi et al., 1977) The yield of mitochondria in 
these preparations was between 3 and 1 1% of the total 
content based on the recovery of enzyme markers (Lai 
and Clark, 1976; Lai et al., 1977) Our preliminary 
studies indicated that the application of these proce- 
dures to single rat brains or brain subregions resulted 
in lower recoveries 
Two new procedures are reported here for the iso- 
lation of mitochondria from rat brain using centrifu- 
gation on a discontinuous Percoll density gradient The 
methods are rapid and produce highly metabolically 
competent mitochondria from single rat forebrain or 
subregions with purity and yields at least as good as 
for other methods currently available The methods 
differ from each other in the composition of the density 
gradient and the handling of fractions prior to the den- 
sity gradient centrifugation, which affects the propor- 
Received October 24, 1989; accepted January 22, 1990 
Address correspondence and reprint requests to Dr N R Sims at 
Department of Medical Biochemistry, Flinders University of South 
Australia, GPO Box 2 100, Adelaide, SA, 500 1, Australia Abbreviations used: CCCP, carbonyl cyanide m-chlorophenylhy- 
drazone; CNPase, 2’,3‘-cyclic nucleotide 3’-phosphodiesterase 
698 ISOLATION OF ACTIVE MITOCHONDRIA FROM RAT BRAIN 699 
tion of mitochondria recovered from small tissue sam- 
ples, the total preparation time, and, to a small extent, 
the degree of contamination of the mitochondria with 
synaptosomes 
Percoll, which consists of colloidal silica coated with 
polyvinylpyrrolidone, has been used widely for the 
preparation of cells and subcellular fractions from other 
tissues, including the rapid isolation of mitochondria 
from rat liver (Reinhart et al., 1982) In the last few 
years, several methods for preparing synaptosomes 
from brain have been described which employ Percoll 
density gradients as part of the procedure (Nagy and 
Delgado-Escueta, 1984; Shank and Campbell, 1984; 
Dunkley et al., 1986, 1988) Mitochondria obtained as 
by-products of some of these preparations have been 
used in several studies (Nagy and Delgado-Escueta, 
1984; Ferrarese et al., 1987), but in these studies the 
isolation conditions were not optimized for the prep- 
aration of mitochondria and there was little charac- 
terization of the properties of the organelles obtained

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. "Two procedures are described for isolating free (nonsynaptosomal) mitochondria from rat brain."
2. "Both procedures employ a discontinuous Percoll gradient and yield well coupled mitochondria which exhibit high rates of respiratory activity and contain little residual contamination by synaptosomes or myelin."
3. "The first method separates mitochondria by gradient centrifugation from a P2 (crude mitochondrial) fraction and is likely to be widely applicable for studies in which at least 500 mg of tissue are available as starting material."
4. "In the second method, the unfractionated homogenate is subjected directly to gradient centrifugation."
5. "This method requires the preparation of more gradients (per gram of tissue) than the first method and yields a subcellular fraction with slightly more synaptosomal contamination."
6. "However, this second procedure is more rapid, requires less manipulation of the tissue, and is suitable for obtaining mitochondria with well preserved metabolic characteristics from subregions of single rat brains."

**Incompleteness:**

- The article does not provide the specific steps for each procedure, only the general overview.
- The article does not mention the specific composition of the density gradient for each method.
- The article does not provide details on the handling of fractions prior to density gradient centrifugation.

**Conclusion:**

The article describes two procedures for isolating mitochondria from rat brain using a discontinuous Percoll gradient. The first method involves gradient centrifugation from a crude mitochondrial fraction and is suitable for studies with at least 500 mg of tissue. The second method involves direct gradient centrifugation of the unfractionated homogenate and is more rapid but yields a subcellular fraction with slightly more synaptosomal contamination. However, the article does not provide specific steps for each procedure or details on the composition of the density gradient and handling of fractions.

summary:
**Summary Iteration 3**:

The Journal of Neurochemistry, published by Raven Press in New York, focuses on the study of neurochemistry. The International Society for Neurochemistry, led by Neil R. Sims from the Department of Medical Biochemistry at Flinders University of South Australia, is actively involved in this field.

This article describes two rapid procedures for isolating metabolically active mitochondria from rat brain using Percoll density gradient centrifugation. The first method, using a P2 fraction, separates mitochondria and is suitable for studies with at least 500 mg of tissue. The second method directly subjects the homogenate to gradient centrifugation and is more rapid but yields mitochondria with slightly more synaptosomal contamination.

These procedures overcome previous technical problems and provide well-coupled mitochondria with high respiratory activity and minimal contamination. The methods produce highly metabolically competent mitochondria from single rat brains or subregions with purity and yields comparable to other available methods.

Percoll, a colloidal silica coated with polyvinylpyrrolidone, is used in these procedures and has been widely used for the preparation of cells and subcellular fractions from various tissues. The field of neurochemistry continues to develop rapid and efficient methods for isolating mitochondria and synaptosomes, with a focus on maintaining the metabolic properties of these organelles and minimizing contamination.

The article also mentions the use of Percoll density gradients in methods for preparing synaptosomes from brain, as well as the rapid isolation of mitochondria from rat liver using Percoll density gradients. These advancements in isolation techniques allow for the recovery of synaptosomes with minimal contamination and the preservation of metabolic characteristics.

Overall, this research contributes to the understanding of neurochemistry by providing effective methods for isolating metabolically active mitochondria from rat brain and subregions. These methods offer improved purity and yield, and can be applied in various studies requiring uncontaminated organelles for analysis. The use of Percoll density gradients plays a crucial role in these procedures, enabling the recovery of mitochondria and synaptosomes with minimal contamination and preserved metabolic properties.

article:
Chunk # 1.

One method specifically developed for the isolation of 
brain mitochondria has made use of Percoll gradient 
centrifugation (Rendon and Masmoudi, 1985) In 
contrast to the preparations described in the present 
article, this procedure involved an initial separation of 
mitochondria from the major contaminants using 
centrifugation on a discontinuous Ficoll gradient and 
produced organelles in which oxidative phosphoryla- 
tion was not well coupled 
A preliminary report of parts of the study reported 
here has been presented previously (Sims, 1989) 
MATERIALS AND METHODS 
Materials 
Male Hooded Wistar rats (230-320 g) from the Department 
of Agriculture (Animal Resources Centre, Gilles Plains, South 
Australia, Australia) were used for all studies Percoll was 
from Pharmacia LKB (Uppsala, Sweden) Sucrose (grade I), 
EDTA (dipotassium salt), Tris, bovine serum albumin (es- 
sentially fatty acid free), cytochrome c, pyruvic acid, malic 
acid, glutamic acid, Triton X-100, 2’,3’-cyclic AMP, and car- 
bony1 cyanide m-chlorophenylhydrazone (CCCP) were from 
Sigma Chemical Co (St Louis, MO, U.S.A.) ADP (K’ salt) 
and NADH were from Boehringer Mannheim (Mannheim, 
F.R.G.) All other chemicals were of analytical grade 
Isolation of mitochondria 
Rats were fasted overnight prior to killing by decapitation 
The brains were rapidly removed (within 1 min) to ice-cold 
“isolation buffer” containing 0.32 M sucrose, I mM EDTA 
(K’ salt), and 10 mM Tris-HC1 (pH 7.4) The cerebellum 
and underlying structures were removed and the remaining 
material used as the “forebrain.” Dissection of subregions, 
when necessary, was carried out rapidly on ice in a cold room 
(4°C) All solutions used were ice-cold, and subsequent ma- 
nipulations of the tissue were carried out on ice The tissue 
was finely minced with scissors in a small amount of isolation 
buffer and washed three times with this buffer The tissue in 
isolation buffer (lo%, wt/vol) was homogenized by hand in 
an all-glass Dounce homogenizer (Kontes, Vineland, NJ, U.S.A.) using four up-and-down strokes with the A pestle 
(total clearance 0.12 mm) and eight strokes with the B pestle 
(total clearance 0.05 mm) Subsequent handling of the ho- 
mogenate varied for the two methods The final procedure 
adopted in each case is outlined below and summarized in 
Fig I A Beckman model 52-2 1 centrifuge at 4°C was used 
for all centrifugation steps All centrifugal forces indicated 
are g,,  Percoll solutions were prepared in isolation buffer, 
and the concentrations are shown as percent (vol/vol) 
Method A The homogenate was centrifuged at 4,000 rpm 
(1,330 g) for 3 min in a Beckman JA 20 rotor The super- 
natant was carefully decanted and the pellet resuspended in 
half of the original volume using a Dounce homogenizer 
This homogenate was recentrifuged as above, the supernatant 
retained, and the pellet discarded The pooled supernatant 
was centrifuged at 16,000 rpm (21,200 g) for 10 rnin in a JA 
20 rotor The decanted supernatant was discarded and the 
pellet resuspended in 15% Percoll (I 0 ml/g of tissue originally 
homogenized) The discontinuous density gradient was pre- 
pared in 16 X 102 mm polyallomer centrifuge tubes by lay- 
ering 3-ml fractions of the resuspended pellet on two pre- 
formed layers consisting of 3.5 ml of 23% Percoll above 3.5 
ml of 40% Percoll (The lower layers were prepared manually 
immediately prior to the start of the preparation by adding 
the 40% Percoll below the 23% Percoll using a 23-gauge lum- 
bar puncture needle and disposable 10-ml syringe.) Tubes 
were centrifuged for 5 rnin at 19,000 rpm (30,700 g) in a JA 
21 rotor (acceleration time: 54 s; deceleration with brake: 
100 s.) Three major bands of material were obtained, and 
the material banding near the interface of the lower two Per- 
coll layers (Fig 1, fraction 3) was diluted 1:4 by gently mixing 
with isolation buffer and then centrifuged at 14,000 rpm 
(16,700 g) for 10 rnin in a JA 21 rotor A firm pellet was not 
obtained because of accumulation at the base of the tube of 
some Percoll camed over from the density gradient The su- 
pernatant was removed with a Pasteur pipette to within a 
few millimeters of the base of the tube and the material gently 
resuspended Fatty acid-free bovine serum albumin (10 mg/ 
ml) was added (0.5 ml/forebrain originally used) and the 
mixture diluted with isolation buffer (final volume 3 ml/fore- 
brain) After centrifugation at 9,000 rpm (6,900 g) for 10 rnin 
in a JA 2 1 rotor, the supernatant was rapidly decanted and 
the pellet gently resuspended in isolation buffer (0.3 ml/fore- 
brain) using a fine Teflon stirring rod This fraction was stored 
on ice for further investigations 
Method B The initial homogenate was mixed 1:l with a 
24% Percoll solution For subregions (less than one cerebral 
hemisphere), a 5% (wt/vol) homogenate was prepared directly 
in 12% Percoll and no further dilution was necessary

mitochondria.protocol:
**Extracted Steps:**

1. Rats were fasted overnight prior to killing by decapitation.
2. The brains were rapidly removed and placed in ice-cold isolation buffer.
3. The cerebellum and underlying structures were removed, and the remaining material used as the "forebrain."
4. The tissue was finely minced with scissors in isolation buffer and washed three times with this buffer.
5. The tissue in isolation buffer (10%, wt/vol) was homogenized using an all-glass Dounce homogenizer.
6. The homogenate was centrifuged at 4,000 rpm (1,330 g) for 3 min.
7. The supernatant was carefully decanted, and the pellet was resuspended in half of the original volume using a Dounce homogenizer.
8. This homogenate was recentrifuged as above, and the supernatant was retained while the pellet was discarded.
9. The pooled supernatant was centrifuged at 16,000 rpm (21,200 g) for 10 min.
10. The decanted supernatant was discarded, and the pellet was resuspended in 15% Percoll.
11. A discontinuous density gradient was prepared in polyallomer centrifuge tubes by layering 3-ml fractions of the resuspended pellet on two preformed layers consisting of 3.5 ml of 23% Percoll above 3.5 ml of 40% Percoll.
12. Tubes were centrifuged for 5 min at 19,000 rpm (30,700 g).
13. Three major bands of material were obtained, and the material banding near the interface of the lower two Percoll layers (fraction 3) was diluted 1:4 with isolation buffer and then centrifuged at 14,000 rpm (16,700 g) for 10 min.
14. The supernatant was removed, and the material was gently resuspended.
15. Fatty acid-free bovine serum albumin was added, and the mixture was diluted with isolation buffer.
16. After centrifugation at 9,000 rpm (6,900 g) for 10 min, the supernatant was decanted, and the pellet was gently resuspended in isolation buffer.
17. This fraction was stored on ice for further investigations.
18. For subregions, a 5% (wt/vol) homogenate was prepared directly in 12% Percoll, and no further dilution was necessary.

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**

- The article does not provide information on the specific volumes of isolation buffer and Percoll used.
- The article does not mention the duration of the centrifugation steps.
- The article does not specify the speed or duration of the centrifugation step in Method B.
- The article does not provide information on the temperature during the isolation process.

**Conclusion:**

The article provides a detailed description of the steps involved in the isolation of brain mitochondria using two different methods (Method A and Method B). However, there are some missing details in the protocol, such as specific volumes, centrifugation durations, and speeds. These missing details may limit the reproducibility of the protocol. Additionally, the article does not mention the temperature during the isolation process, which is important for maintaining mitochondrial integrity. Overall, while the article provides a good starting point for mitochondrial isolation, additional information is needed to fully replicate the protocol.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for isolating mitochondria from rat brain using Percoll gradient centrifugation:

1. Homogenize brain tissue in isolation buffer (0.32 M sucrose, 1 mM EDTA, 10 mM Tris-HCl, pH 7.4).
2. Centrifuge the homogenate at 1,330 g for 3 minutes.
3. Recentrifuge the supernatant at 21,200 g for 10 minutes to obtain a pellet (P2 fraction).
4. Homogenize the pellet in Percoll.
5. Layer the homogenate on a Percoll gradient consisting of 23% Percoll and 40% Percoll.
6. Centrifuge the gradient at 30,700 g for 5 minutes.
7. Collect the fraction accumulating near the interface of the lower two layers (fraction 3).
8. Wash fraction 3 in isolation buffer at 16,700 g for 10 minutes.
9. Rewash fraction 3 in isolation buffer plus serum albumin at 6,900 g for 10 minutes.
10. Dilute fraction 3 with an equal volume of 24% Percoll and layer on a Percoll gradient.
11. Centrifuge the gradient at 30,700 g for 5 minutes.
12. Wash fraction 3 in isolation buffer at 16,700 g for 10 minutes.
13. Rewash fraction 3 in isolation buffer plus serum albumin at 7,300 g for 10 minutes.

Notes:
- The article does not provide complete details for all steps of the protocol.
- It is unclear how the homogenate in Percoll is prepared and the specific concentrations of Percoll used.
- The alternative procedure for small samples is not described in detail.
- The article mentions the storage of the loose pellet from the first spin on ice, but does not provide further information on the handling of the stored pellet.
- The article does not specify the volumes used for the aliquots of homogenate or the dilution of fraction 3 with isolation buffer.

Conclusion:
The article provides a general outline of the mitochondrial isolation protocol using Percoll gradient centrifugation. However, there are several missing details and uncertainties regarding specific concentrations, volumes, and handling of the samples. Further clarification is needed to fully replicate the protocol.

summary: Revised Summary: Researchers isolated mitochondria from rat brain using Percoll gradient centrifugation. They layered homogenate aliquots in Percoll gradients in polyallomer centrifuge tubes and collected the fraction near the interface of the lower two layers. After centrifugation at 19,000 rpm for 5 minutes, a loose pellet was formed. For small samples, the loose pellet was stored on ice. Suitable aliquots were transferred for respiratory measurements or enzyme assays. The total protein content was determined using the Lowry method. The experiment involved the use of Eppendorf conical microfuge tubes and a JA 21 rotor. Bovine serum albumin and fatty acid-free bovine serum albumin were added to the samples. The respiratory activity of mitochondria from brain subregions was measured using a Clark-type oxygen electrode. The addition of CCCP uncoupled the respiration. Triton X-100 was used in the enzyme assays. The COBAS Bio centrifugal analyzer was used to simultaneously assay multiple samples. NADH, sodium pyruvate, and Triethanolamine were included in the assay buffers. Cytochrome c oxidase and 2',3'-cyclic nucleotide 3'-phosphodiesterase activity were measured. The total protein content was determined using the Lowry method. Polyallomer centrifuge tubes were used to separate different components of a mixture. Fatty acid-free bovine serum albumin was added to prevent interference from fatty acids. Sodium pyruvate was included as a substrate for respiratory measurements. Aliquots of the homogenate were taken for further analysis. The homogenate was centrifuged to separate different layers based on density gradients. The interface between the lower two layers was of particular interest. The pellet obtained from the first spin was stored on ice. Suitable aliquots for respiratory measurements or enzyme assays were transferred for analysis. The homogenate was prepared in isolation buffer. Centrifugation steps were performed to isolate mitochondria from brain subregions. The oxygen uptake by mitochondria and enzyme activities were measured. Total protein determination was also carried out.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The crude mitochondrial (P2) pellet was resuspended in isolation buffer containing 15% Percoll.
2. The resuspended pellet was placed over a middle layer containing Percoll at concentrations between 20 and 28%.
3. The middle layer had been layered previously above 40% Percoll.
4. The three-step gradient was centrifuged at 30,700 g for 5 min.
5. After centrifugation, the gradient resulted in:
   a. A dense cream-colored band near the top of the gradient (fraction 1).
   b. A broad band near the interfaces between the upper two layers (fraction 2).
   c. A narrower band near the interface of the lower two layers (fraction 3).
   d. A small amount of material near the base of the tube (fraction 4).
6. Fraction 3 was highly enriched in mitochondria.
7. If the P2 pellet was resuspended in isolation buffer containing no Percoll prior to the gradient centrifugation stage, most of the material accumulated at the first interface with little penetration of organelles into the Percoll-containing layers.
8. Resuspending the P2 pellet in 15% Percoll overcame this problem by ensuring that a large proportion of the contaminating material migrated to the top of the gradient during centrifugation.
9. The concentration of 40% Percoll was chosen for the lowest layer to reduce the possibility of damage during pelleting and resuspension.
10. The final choice of the concentration of Percoll in the middle layer was based on estimates of the relative recovery of mitochondria compared with the activity of lactate dehydrogenase.
11. The recovery of mitochondria was estimated from the oxygen uptake rates determined under comparable conditions for washed fraction 3 and unfractionated homogenates.
12. The recovery of mitochondria was measured after addition of the uncoupling agent, CCCP, and after addition of ADP, which does not readily cross synaptosomal membranes.
13. A Percoll concentration of 23% was chosen for subsequent studies as it provided a good compromise between the proportion of mitochondrial activity recovered and contamination of the fraction.
14. Minor modifications of the initial centrifugation steps and washing procedures improved the yield and recovery of the mitochondria-enriched fraction.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation using Percoll density gradient centrifugation. The steps include resuspending the P2 pellet in isolation buffer containing 15% Percoll, layering it over a middle layer with varying Percoll concentrations, and centrifuging the gradient. The fractions obtained are described, with fraction 3 being highly enriched in mitochondria. However, some protocol details are missing, such as the specific composition of the isolation buffer and the washing procedures.

summary: High concentrations of Percoll produce measurable color in this assay. Protein content of fractions from gradients was determined by subtracting "blank" values prepared with equivalent fractions of gradients centrifuged with buffer containing no tissue. Electron microscopy was performed on samples of the final resuspended pellet using a conical embedding capsule. Fixation and embedding were done according to the procedure of Mason et al. The pellet was stained, dehydrated, and embedded in Epon. Method A involved resuspending the crude mitochondrial pellet in isolation buffer containing Percoll and layering it over a middle layer of Percoll. Centrifugation resulted in the formation of distinct bands in the gradient. Fraction 3 was found to be highly enriched in mitochondria. Resuspending the pellet in 15% Percoll improved the penetration of organelles into the Percoll-containing layers. The choice of Percoll concentration in the middle layer was based on estimates of mitochondrial recovery. A concentration of 23% was chosen for subsequent studies. Minor modifications to the centrifugation and washing procedures improved the yield and quality of the mitochondria-enriched fraction.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Isolation of Active Mitochondria from Rat Brain**

1. The distributions of three marker enzymes and total protein in the three major fractions of the gradient compared to the initial homogenate and P2 fraction are shown in Figure 3. Fraction 4 (at the base of the tube) contained less than 0.5% of the total protein, and reliable estimates of the marker enzymes were not able to be obtained in this fraction.
2. The mitochondria1 marker enzyme cytochrome c oxidase was highly enriched in fraction 3, whereas lactate dehydrogenase was mostly in fractions 1 and 2, and the myelin marker CNPase in fraction 1.
3. The recovery (relative to the initial homogenate) of the marker enzymes in fraction 3 prior to washing was 12.1 ± 0.7% for cytochrome c oxidase, 0.32 ± 0.05% for lactate dehydrogenase, and 0.11 ± 0.04% for CNPase.
4. The oxygen uptake rates of the final washed preparation from fraction 3 determined in the presence of various substrates are shown in Table 1.
5. The respiration rates, respiratory control ratios, and ADP/O ratio obtained indicate that fraction 3 was highly enriched with metabolically active, well-coupled mitochondria.
6. Measurement of the respiratory activity of these mitochondria in the presence of bovine serum albumin resulted in a small increase of the respiratory control ratio to 7.3 ± 0.5 (with pyruvate plus malate as substrates) primarily due to a lower rate of state 4 respiration.
7. Based on the oxygen uptake rates with pyruvate plus malate as substrates (no bovine serum albumin present), the final recovery using two forebrains as starting material was 10.4 ± 0.7% of total (CCCP-uncoupled) activity and 13.2 ± 0.7% of the activity in the presence of ADP with a 6.3-fold enrichment of activity compared to the initial homogenate.
8. Lactate dehydrogenase was reduced to 0.11 ± 0.02% of the total activity in the final washed preparation.
9. Electron micrographs revealed that mitochondria, identified from the presence of a double membrane and cristae, clearly accounted for most of the material in the final preparation.
10. In all preparations, there was a small proportion of nonmitochondrial, membrane-bound structures which were probably synaptosomes, based on the presence of vesicles and, in many cases, enclosed mitochondria. Occasional unattached membrane without inclusions was also seen.
11. Most preparations examined also contained some small bead-like structures, presumably Percoll, which were incompletely removed even though the preparation was washed three times between the gradient centrifugation and fixation.
12. Electron micrographs of the mitochondrial fraction prepared by method A are shown in Figure 4A.

summary: Revised Summary: The study aimed to isolate active mitochondria from rat brain and analyze their respiratory properties. The distribution of protein and marker enzymes in different fractions obtained through density gradient centrifugation was examined. The final washed preparation from fraction 3 was found to be highly enriched with metabolically active mitochondria. The respiratory activity of these mitochondria, measured using various substrates, indicated their efficiency. Electron micrographs confirmed the presence of mitochondria, along with other structures, in the final preparation. Lactate dehydrogenase, oxygen, CCCP, ADP, 2', cytochrome c, pyruvate, and respiratory control are important factors in mitochondrial function. State 3, state 4, uncoupled ratio, and ADP/O ratio are key states that determine mitochondrial respiration. Malate, glutamate, and succinate are respiratory substrates used in metabolic studies. SEM is used to analyze the results of neurochemical preparations. Myelin and CNPase are markers used to identify specific fractions. Cytochrome c oxidase is highly enriched in fraction 3. Bovine serum albumin is used to measure respiratory activity. Electron micrographs reveal the presence of mitochondria and other structures. Lactate dehydrogenase activity is measured in the presence of CCCP or ADP. The recovery and relative values of marker enzymes and oxygen uptake rates are assessed. Percoll is used for isolation and fixation of synaptosomes and vesicles. The final recovery of mitochondrial fraction activity is 6.3-fold enriched compared to the initial homogenate.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for Isolation of Mitochondria from Rat Brain:

1. Prepare a homogenate of rat brain tissue.
2. Centrifuge the homogenate through Percoll density gradients.
3. Remove fraction 3 from the gradient after centrifugation.
4. Wash fraction 3.
5. Measure the recovery of lactate dehydrogenase activity and oxygen uptake activity in fraction 3.
6. Repeat the isolation process using single rat brains or half brains.
7. Use a 1.5-ml Eppendorf centrifuge tube for the final centrifugation step.
8. Divide the half-brain homogenate in two prior to isolation to obtain a lower recovery.
9. Explore the possibility of isolating brain mitochondria without initial preparation of a P2 pellet.
10. Analyze the fractions obtained through Percoll density gradients.
11. Determine the effect of altering the Percoll concentration in the middle band.
12. Use Percoll concentration of 26% in the middle band for subsequent studies.
13. Assess the respiratory properties and marker enzyme activities of the washed mitochondrial fraction.
14. Isolate mitochondria from hippocampus, striatum, and neocortex using Method B.
15. Include bovine serum albumin in the buffer for final resuspension of the samples.

**Incompleteness:** The article does not provide specific details on the homogenization process, washing steps, or the composition of the buffers used.

**Conclusion:** The article provides a detailed protocol for isolating mitochondria from rat brain using Percoll density gradients. The steps mentioned cover the overall procedure, but there are gaps in the protocol regarding specific details. Additional information is needed on the homogenization process, washing steps, and buffer composition to fully replicate the isolation protocol.

summary:
Summary (Iteration 4):

Researchers used Percoll density gradients to isolate a mitochondria-enriched fraction from rat brains. They investigated the effects of SIMS, CCCP, and ADP on oxygen uptake and respiration rates in the isolated mitochondria. Recovery rates were slightly lower when using single rat brains compared to half brains. They developed Method B, which allows for the isolation of brain mitochondria without the initial preparation of a P2 pellet. Altering the Percoll concentration in the middle band affected the recovery of mitochondrial respiratory activity, with a compromise between recovery and lactate dehydrogenase contamination. The isolated mitochondria were metabolically active and well coupled, with low levels of synaptosomal contamination and free membrane. Oxygen uptake rates and enzyme activities were stabilized using bovine serum albumin in the buffer. Electron micrographs confirmed the presence of smaller mitochondria in the hippocampus, striatum, and neocortex. Marker enzymes, such as cytochrome c oxidase and CNPase, were used to assess the purity and activity of isolated mitochondria. Fractionation techniques using Eppendorf centrifuge tubes and microfuges were employed to isolate mitochondria. Lactate dehydrogenase contamination and recovery rates were considered in the isolation process. Percoll density gradients were used to separate mitochondria from other components. Bovine serum albumin was used to stabilize smaller mitochondria during handling. The specific activity of marker enzymes, such as pyruvate and malate, was compared between the initial homogenate and isolated mitochondria. The results were published in J Neurochem, Vol 55, No 2, 1990, and presented in figures, tables, and SEM values.

This problem presumably resulted from an interaction

DISCUSSION 
Measurements of respiratory properties, the activity 
of marker enzymes, and the electron micrographs in- 
dicate that both procedures described in this article 
produce a fraction which is highly enriched in mito- 
chondria that are metabolically well preserved These 
methods offer several advantages over other procedures 
which are currently widely used for the isolation of free 
brain mitochondria Because of the short duration of 
the centrifugation and, for method B, the elimination 
of the preparation of the P2 fraction, these procedures 
are considerably more rapid Furthermore, the mito- 
chondria are isolated without the need for an ultracen- 
trifuge or a swing-out rotor, and the steps which require 
pelleting and resuspension of fractions containing mi- 
tochondria have been reduced, greatly decreasing the 
potential for damage during isolation 
Contamination of the final mitochondrial fraction 
with either synaptosomes or myelin was low, based on 
the activities of lactate dehydrogenase and CNPase 
For method B, the ratio of recovery of mitochondrial 
markers (respiratory activity or cytochrome c oxidase) 
to lactate dehydrogenase was 54, which compares fa- 
vorably with ratios of 25-43 reported for free mito- 
chondria isolated using Ficoll density gradient centrif- 
ugation (Lai and Clark, 1976; Lai et al., 1977) This 
ratio for preparations obtained by method A was 95, indicating an even lower degree of contamination with 
synaptosomes 
The specific rate of oxygen uptake (relative to pro- 
tein) and the relative enrichment of the mitochondrial 
markers compared to the initial homogenate was ap- 
proximately twofold higher than most values reported 
previously for brain mitochondria (Clark and Nicklas, 
1970; Lai and Clark, 1976, 1979; Lai et al., 1977; Hil- 
lered et al., 1984; Rendon and Masmoudi, 1985) This 
apparent difference perhaps could be explained by 
variations in the methods for measuring oxygen uptake 
and enzyme activities and, particularly, in the appli- 
cation of standard procedures for total protein esti- 
mation However, samples of mitochondria prepared 
in parallel experiments by a procedure based on Ficoll 
gradient fractionation (Lai et al., 1977; Lai and Clark, 
1979) produced values under the assay conditions of 
our laboratory of 9 1 f 6 and 14.7 f 0.5 nmol of 02/ 
min/mg of protein for state 3 and state 4 respiration, 
respectively (n = 4; pyruvate plus malate as substrates) 
These results are very similar to the published values 
for this preparation [state 3: 79 nmol of Oz/min/mg 
of protein; state 4: 15 nmol of 02/min/mg of protein 
(Lai et al., 1977)l Thus, the higher activities obtained 
for mitochondria isolated using Percoll density gra- 
dients may truly reflect differences in preservation of 
the organelles or the purity of the final preparation 
Interestingly, synaptosomes isolated using a rapid pro- 
cedure based on discontinuous Percoll density gradients 
(Dunkley et al., 1988) have been shown to have higher 
specific activities for the release and uptake of various 
neurotransmitters compared with preparations ob- 
tained using Ficoll or sucrose density gradients (Thorne 
et al., 1988; Waterman and Sims, 1989) 
The choice of 5 min for the density gradient cen- 
trifugation was made because of the successes achieved 
with such centrifugation periods in isolating synapto- 
somes (Dunkley et al., 1986, 1988) As with the syn- 
aptosomal preparations, both the sedimentation coef- 
ficient and density of the subcellular components will 
contribute to the separation achieved during this short 
period The composition of the fractions obtained could 
be affected by changes in conditions, including the an- 
gle of the rotor used and its acceleration and deceler- 
ation times Thus, small adjustments of the Percoll 
concentration of the middle layer of the discontinuous 
gradient may be necessary for optimal separation of 
mitochondria if different equipment is used

3. "Furthermore, the mitochondria are isolated without the need for an ultracentrifuge or a swing-out rotor, and the steps which require pelleting and resuspension of fractions containing mitochondria have been reduced, greatly decreasing the potential for damage during isolation."
4. "Contamination of the final mitochondrial fraction with either synaptosomes or myelin was low, based on the activities of lactate dehydrogenase and CNPase."
5. "For method B, the ratio of recovery of mitochondrial markers (respiratory activity or cytochrome c oxidase) to lactate dehydrogenase was 54, which compares favorably with ratios of 25-43 reported for free mitochondria isolated using Ficoll density gradient centrifugation."
6. "The specific rate of oxygen uptake (relative to protein) and the relative enrichment of the mitochondrial markers compared to the initial homogenate was approximately twofold higher than most values reported previously for brain mitochondria."
7. 8. "The choice of 5 min for the density gradient centrifugation was made because of the successes achieved with such centrifugation periods in isolating synaptosomes."

The article provides some information on the advantages of the procedures used for mitochondrial isolation and the quality of the isolated mitochondria. Oxygen uptake rates were measured using pyruvate and malate as substrates. The final mitochondrial fraction had minimal contamination from synaptosomes or myelin. Density gradient centrifugation was used to separate particles based on density. The methods used showed higher oxygen uptake rates compared to previous studies. Marker enzymes and respiratory properties were assessed to ensure the quality of the mitochondrial fraction. The choice of centrifugation period was based on successful outcomes in isolating synaptosomes. (Reference: Lai et al.)

Chunk # 7.
Even with 
identical conditions, alterations of the content of Per- 
coll in the middle band may be advantageous for some 
preparations in which greater yield (at the expense of 
purity) or even greater purity (with reduced yield) is 
desirable 
The respiratory properties of mitochondria isolated 
by method B from brain subregions were very similar 
to those obtained when larger samples were used as 
starting material No assays of respiratory activity of 
the initial homogenates were performed for these 
subregions in order to maximize the quantity of or- 
J Neuruchem., Vul 55, No 2, 1990 706 N R SIMS 
ganelles isolated However, estimated recoveries of free 
mitochondria based on previously determined values 
for ADP-stimulated oxygen uptake by homogenates of 
these subregions (Sims et al., 1986) were 11.1% for 
neocortex, 9.9% for striatum, and 10.3% for hippo- 
campus, indicating that the yield of active free mito- 
chondria was little reduced when these subregions were 
used as starting material Direct estimates of recoveries 
based on cytochrome c oxidase activity in separate 
studies using even smaller samples (approximately 50 
mg of striatum or 120 mg of neocortex) were between 
5 and 14% (Sims, in preparation), further confirming 
the usefulness of this procedure for obtaining mito- 
chondria from small tissue samples 
Several other studies of mitochondria isolated from 
brain subregions have been reported recently (Dagani 
et al., 1988; Marzatico et al., 1988; Thakar and Hassan, 
1988a,b) For some of these studies (Thakar and Has- 
san, 1988a,b), the preparative procedure was based only 
on differential centrifugation (without a density gra- 
dient step) and presumably resulted in a fraction highly 
contaminated with nonmitochondrial subcellular 
fractions Coupled respiration can be readily demon- 
strated in such preparations However, for many stud- 
ies, such a preparation offers little or no advantage over 
equivalent measurements obtained from unfraction- 
ated tissue homogenates (Sims and Blass, 1986) In- 
deed, the latter more accurately reflects the response 
of the total population of mitochondria in the tissue, 
and the possibility of damage during preparation is 
greatly reduced 
Mitochondrial fractions which are largely free of 
contamination are essential for obtaining unambiguous 
measurements of many important mitochondrial 
properties Preparations of suitable purity generally 
have only been obtained from brain tissue using pro- 
cedures employing density gradient centrifugation Of 
the other studies examining mitochondria from brain 
subregions, only the detailed investigations of Dagani 
et al ( 1988), which used Ficoll density gradients based 
on the method of Lai et al (1977), provided sufficient 
information to allow the mitochondrial recovery to be 
estimated The recovery was at least twice that obtained 
for subregions using method B, as described in the 
present article, and indeed was also more than twice 
the yield obtained with much larger amounts of starting 
material in the original studies of Lai and co-workers 
(1977) The use of mechanical rather than manual ho- 
mogenization may have contributed to the higher yield 
which was achieved However, this impressive recovery 
apparently was obtained at the expense of purity of the 
mitochondria, as indicated by the ratio of recovery of 
the mitochondrial marker citrate synthase to lactate 
dehydrogenase (6.3-6.5), the lower specific rates of ox- 
ygen uptake, and the high protein content of the mi- 
tochondrial fraction relative to that of the synaptosomal 
fraction 
In conclusion, the two methods described in the 
present article offer considerable improvements over procedures described previously in allowing rapid mi- 
tochondrial isolation without compromising the purity 
or metabolic properties of the final preparation 
Method B is the procedure of choice for samples from 
brain subregions or in situations in which it is consid- 
ered important to minimize manipulation or increase 
the speed of isolation Method A requires the use of 
only half as many gradients (per gram of tissue) and 
yields a slightly less contaminated preparation than 
method B Therefore, method A is more suitable for 
routine preparations when larger amounts of tissue are 
available 
Acknowledgment: This work was supported by the National 
Health and Medical Research Foundation (Australia) The 
author thanks Mr S A Jenkins for his skilled technical 
assistance, The assistance of Mr K Gascoigne and Dr 
J H T Power in producing the electron micrographs is 
gratefully acknowledged

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. "The two methods described in the present article offer considerable improvements over procedures described previously in allowing rapid mitochondrial isolation without compromising the purity or metabolic properties of the final preparation."

2. "Method B is the procedure of choice for samples from brain subregions or in situations in which it is considered important to minimize manipulation or increase the speed of isolation."

3. "Method A requires the use of only half as many gradients (per gram of tissue) and yields a slightly less contaminated preparation than method B. Therefore, method A is more suitable for routine preparations when larger amounts of tissue are available."

**Conclusion:**
The article discusses two methods (Method A and Method B) for mitochondrial isolation. Method B is recommended for samples from brain subregions or situations where minimizing manipulation or increasing isolation speed is important. Method A is suitable for routine preparations with larger tissue amounts. The article does not provide a detailed step-by-step protocol for either method, but it emphasizes the improvements and advantages of these methods over previous procedures.

summary:
Summary: Iteration 4
1. Alterations of Per-coll content in the middle band can be advantageous for some preparations, allowing for greater yield or purity depending on the desired outcome.
2. Mitochondria isolated from brain subregions using Method B show similar respiratory properties to larger samples, indicating the effectiveness of this method.
3. The yield of active free mitochondria from brain subregions is not significantly reduced when using smaller samples, as estimated based on previous studies.
4. Some studies on mitochondria isolated from brain subregions have resulted in contaminated fractions, while others offer little advantage over unfractionated tissue homogenates.
5. Preparations of mitochondria largely free of contamination are crucial for accurate measurements of mitochondrial properties.
6. The two methods described in the article provide improvements in mitochondrial isolation without compromising purity or metabolic properties.
7. Method B, developed by S A Jenkins, K Gascoigne, and J H T Power, is the preferred procedure for isolating mitochondria from brain tissue, involving mechanical homogenization and Ficoll density gradients.
8. The recovery of mitochondria using method B is higher compared to method A, but the mitochondrial fractions may have some contamination from nonmitochondrial subcellular fractions.
9. Coupled respiration can be demonstrated in the mitochondrial fractions obtained using method B.
10. Method A, also developed by S A Jenkins, K Gascoigne, and J H T Power, requires fewer gradients but is less efficient than method B, making it suitable for routine preparations with larger amounts of tissue.
11. Larger tissue samples are recommended for routine preparations using method B to maximize efficiency.
12. The neocortex and its important mitochondrial properties are studied using the marker citrate synthase.
13. Recovery and purity trade-off is observed in the isolation process, with mechanical homogenization contributing to higher yield but lower purity.
14. The respiratory properties of mitochondria isolated by Method B can be assessed through assays of respiratory activity.
15. Preparations largely free of contamination demonstrate coupled respiration.
16. Detailed investigations by Dagani et al. (1988) provide further insights into the recovery and purity of mitochondrial fractions.
17. The work was supported by the National Health and Medical Research Foundation, and the author acknowledges the technical assistance and contributions of S. A. Jenkins, K. Gascoigne, and J. H. T. Power.
18. References: Chance and Williams (1956), Clark and Nicklas (1970), Dagani et al. (1988), Dunkley et al. (1986), Lai et al. (1977), Sims et al., Marzatico et al., Thakar and Hassan, Sims and Blass.

topics.topic: Rapid Percoll gradient procedure for isolating synaptosomes directly from an S fraction

topics.topic: Mitochondrial metabolism following bilateral cerebral ischemia in the gerbil
topics.justification: This topic investigates the changes in mitochondrial metabolism after cerebral ischemia, providing insights into the effects of ischemia on brain energy metabolism.

topics.topic: Method for quantitation of protein in the presence of Percoll
topics.justification: This topic presents a method for accurately quantifying protein in the presence of Percoll, which is a commonly used gradient material in biological research.

topics.topic: Preparation and properties of mitochondria derived from synaptosomes
topics.justification: This topic discusses the preparation and properties of mitochondria derived from synaptosomes, providing insights into the characteristics of synaptic mitochondria.

topics.topic: Isolation and characterization of synaptic and nonsynaptic mitochondria from rat brain
topics.justification: This topic focuses on the isolation and characterization of both synaptic and nonsynaptic mitochondria from rat brain, contributing to the understanding of mitochondrial heterogeneity in the brain.

topics.topic: Protein measurements with the Fohn phenol reagent
